Land Rover Freelander 3.2 i6 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Freelander 3.2 i6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 3.2 i6 (306PN) engine typically costs £2,200–£3,500 supply only. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 depending on workshop location. Main dealer new engines are no longer available — specialist reconditioning is the route.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Freelander 3.2 i6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the Volvo-sourced SI6 is widely regarded as very reliable. It has no DPF or EGR system to fail, and its naturally aspirated design means fewer components to go wrong than a turbocharged engine. However, it's now an older engine, so age-related issues like alternator pulley wear, PCV diaphragm failure, and timing chain tensioner wear are common.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Freelander 3.2 i6?

acceptedAnswer.text: Alternator drive pulley failure (whining noise), PCV diaphragm failure (whistling noise, oil consumption), and timing chain tensioner wear (rattle on cold start). The engine also suffers from high fuel consumption — expect 25–27 MPG combined.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Freelander 3.2 i6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Land Rover scrap threshold: if your Freelander is worth £4,500 and a recon engine is £2,800 (62%), it's borderline — assess the rest of the vehicle carefully. If the vehicle is a tired £3,000 example, the same engine exceeds 60% — get a scrap valuation first. Collectors and enthusiasts often justify the spend due to the rarity of the petrol straight-six.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Freelander 3.2 i6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 3.2 i6 uses the 306PN, also known as the Volvo B6324S or SI6. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the alternator pulley, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The alternator pulley itself can be replaced (around £200–£400). It's a much cheaper fix than a full alternator replacement. If you hear any whining or screeching from the engine bay, have the alternator drive pulley inspected promptly.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Freelander 3.2 i6 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available — specialist reconditioning is the route

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the only engine generation this badge ever used — the 306PN (B6324S). See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Freelander 3.2 i6 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The transverse-mounted layout makes access tighter than in longitudinal installations, so labour can be higher than on some other Land Rover models.

cta.label: → Compare Freelander 3.2 i6 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Alternator Drive Pulley Failure
problems.symptoms: Whining or screeching noise from the engine bay, battery warning light, intermittent charging issues, rough idle.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, the pulley can seize and snap the belt.
problems.recommendation: This is one of the most reported issues on the 3.2 i6. The alternator pulley develops play in its shaft, causing a whining noise. It's a straightforward fix, but if ignored, the pulley can fail completely, leaving you with a dead battery and potentially a snapped serpentine belt. If you hear any whining or screeching, have the alternator drive pulley inspected promptly.

problems.title: PCV / Crankcase Ventilation Diaphragm Failure
problems.symptoms: Whistling noise from under the bonnet, increased oil consumption, oil mist in the intake, crankcase fault codes.
problems.recommendation: The PCV diaphragm on the SI6 engine can split or fail, causing oil mist to be drawn into the intake, increased oil consumption, and a characteristic whistling noise. It's a relatively inexpensive part to replace, but if ignored, it can lead to oil contamination of the intake system and potential running issues.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking, loss of power, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if the chain snaps, it's an interference engine — catastrophic failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: at first sign of rattle, book inspection today.

problems.recommendation: Like many chain-driven engines, the 3.2 i6's timing chain tensioners can wear over time. This can cause the chain to stretch or rattle, particularly on cold starts. If you hear any metallic rattle from the engine on startup, book an inspection immediately. While less common than on some other engines, a snapped chain would be catastrophic for this interference engine.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Freelander 3.2 i6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Simple, cheap fix — do it preventatively
Inexpensive repair that prevents oil consumption
Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Viable if the rest of the vehicle is solid; often cheaper to fit a good used head

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 3.2 i6? Check These First
buyIf: * No whining or screeching from the alternator pulley area
* No whistling or hissing from the engine (PCV diaphragm intact)
* Full service history with regular oil changes
* No history of overheating — this engine needs a healthy cooling system
avoidIf: * Whining noise from the engine bay — alternator pulley failure
* Whistling or hissing — PCV diaphragm likely split
* Rattling on cold start — timing chain tensioner wear
* Overheating or coolant loss — head gasket or cracked head risk

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 3.2 i6 is one of the more reliable petrol engines Land Rover fitted to the Freelander, but it's now over a decade old. With no DPF or EGR to worry about, it offers a simpler ownership proposition than modern diesels. However, fuel consumption is high (around 25–27 MPG combined), and the tightly packed engine bay means some jobs take longer. A £2,800 reconditioned engine on a Freelander valued at £4,500 is 62% — at the Land Rover scrap threshold. On a sub-£3,500 example, think carefully before committing.

cta.label: → Compare Freelander 3.2 i6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Freelander 3.2 i6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle on startup (timing chain tensioner wear)
* Service History — Regular oil changes using the correct 0W-30 or 5W-30 spec
* Alternator Pulley — Check for whining or screeching noise; any play in the pulley?
* Whistling Noise — Listen under bonnet — PCV diaphragm may be split
* Cooling System — Check coolant level and condition — overheating kills these engines
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? Could be PCV or piston rings
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (unlikely on a petrol) and corrosion
